responses.response: The claim that S4 'raises the floor without lowering the ceiling' ignores whose floor and whose ceiling: the floor being raised is measured by pupil outcomes, but the workload floor for general-track teachers is simultaneously being lowered — they absorb more remedial and diagnostic labor while selective-track teachers keep smaller, more homogeneous classes and stable conditions indefinitely. Non-disruptiveness for institutions is not the same as non-disruptiveness for the workforce administering the compensation. If proponents of A17 want this to be a genuine non-disruptive equilibrium rather than a permanent asymmetry, they must accept binding class-size parity between selective and general tracks as part of the 'floor-raising' — otherwise the claim of institutional preservation is really a claim of teacher-burden preservation dressed as pluralism.

The near-certain non-state offset does not just weaken S2's equity case in the abstract — it directly confirms my workload objection: state-sector teachers absorb a decade of parallel-regime administrative burden for an aggregate equity gain the scenario itself concedes may be 'minimal or even negative.' This is not a reason to soften my opposition; it sharpens it. If the offset is as well-documented as A6 states, then the burden-to-benefit ratio for teachers is even worse than a contested-evidence scenario would suggest — we are asked to bear near-certain costs for near-uncertain gains. I would only revise toward conditional support if S2 were paired with binding non-state quota alignment (removing the offset channel) or radically shortened timelines, which A6 doesn't itself provide but which strengthens the case for my existing conditions.

A10's structural argument that S3 is 'the only structurally coherent equity option' is one I largely share, but it treats choice-architecture and maintainer-transparency safeguards as sufficient conditions for viability while my position insists teacher capacity is an equally load-bearing pillar, not a secondary implementation detail. Safeguards against middle-class flight address where pupils go; they do nothing to address whether the teachers receiving the more heterogeneous cohorts can actually teach them well. A10's own uncertainty acknowledgment — that teacher-retraining cost/capacity has 'no precise per-cohort figure' — is exactly the gap my position flags as decision-critical, not incidental. I don't oppose A10's structural logic; I maintain that without costed, legislated, pre-rollout teacher capacity guarantees standing alongside the choice-architecture safeguards A10 names, the 'structural coherence' is theoretical rather than deliverable.
